title: Atlas (codebase map)
depends: core Â· diagrams Â· code-intel Â· recap

meta.value: core Â· diagrams Â· code-intel Â· recap

blocks.heading: The visual-atlas product â€” it scans a repo, groups modules into domains, drafts a two-level map, and renders `atlas.html` plus a page per domain.

rows.value: The atlas **block model** (the `AtlasBlock` union â€” `atlas-tldr`, `domain-map`, `domain-index`, `domain-tldr`, `components`, `depth`, `owns`, `seams`, `diagram-section`) and the `atlas.domains.json` **grouping config**.

A standing onboarding map: the atlas page gives the all-domains picture, each `domain-<slug>/` page is a deep-dive + reference. Mechanical scan handles the inventory; a human writes the prose and the *why*.
The page you are reading **is** this domain. The very generator documented below produced it â€” the scanner drafted these cards, then a human enriched them.

bigIdea.label: The load-bearing idea
bigIdea.line: "Hybrid â€” the scanner does the inventory, the human writes the domains and the why."
bigIdea.sub: `scanInventory` finds modules, imports, and exports; `firstGuessConfig`/`reconcile` group them into domains from `atlas.domains.json` globs and report **drift** (never auto-applies it); `buildAtlasDraft`/`buildDomainDraft` emit a skeleton with empty `purpose`/`detail` fields. The draft is written **only when absent** (never clobbers authored prose unless `--force`), and `lint-atlas` flags the blank fields a human still needs to fill.

blocks.title: The pieces
blocks.intro: One CLI (three modes) over five library modules: a grouping config with drift detection, a scanner that builds drafts, the block model that types the page, an assembler that renders HTML, and a completeness lint. Tap a card to jump to its full section.

cards.name: CLI
cards.purpose: `bin/atlas.ts` â€” the entry with three modes: **full scan** (config + drift + draft-when-absent + render), **single-domain** (rescan one page), and **render-only** (`--all` / `--blocks`). Lays out `atlas.*` at the top and each domain in its own `domain-<slug>/` folder.

cards.name: config + drift
cards.purpose: `atlas-config.ts` â€” the human-owned `atlas.domains.json`: one domain per `globs` set. `firstGuessConfig` seeds it (one domain per srcRoot subdir); `reconcile` refills resolved `modules` and reports drift â€” unassigned modules, stale paths, empty domains.

cards.name: scan + draft
cards.purpose: `gather-atlas.ts` â€” walks srcRoots, routes each module to the TS/JS or Python parser, aggregates cross-domain edges, and emits the atlas + domain draft documents (with the mechanical domain-map diagram).

cards.name: block model
cards.purpose: `atlas-blocks.ts` â€” the `AtlasBlock` union that types every page, plus page-option shapes (`AtlasOpts`/`DomainOpts`), the layer-color table, and the id-uniqueness / diagram-collection helpers. **This very file is documented below.**

cards.name: render
cards.purpose: `assemble-atlas.ts` â€” turns a block list into a self-contained HTML page: `assembleAtlas` for the map, `assembleDomain` for a domain page. Renders each `diagram-section`/`depth` diagram via the diagrams domain.

cards.name: completeness lint
cards.purpose: `lint-atlas.ts` â€” the demo-standard floor. Warns (via `onWarn`, never errors) when a page skips the lead, the map, the index, the components, an internal-arch diagram when large, or the seams â€” and flags scanner-drafted tiles with no purpose.

blocks.title: How a full scan runs
blocks.intro: `gather-atlas` is the hub. The repo is scanned into an `Inventory`; `atlas-config` groups modules into domains (first-guess or reconcile, reporting drift); `buildAtlasDraft`/`buildDomainDraft` emit skeleton docs **only when absent**; a human enriches the blank `purpose`/`detail` fields; then `assembleAtlas`/`assembleDomain` render `atlas.html` + each `domain-<slug>/` page â€” with `lint-atlas` gating the completeness floor.

diagram.caption: Drift is **reported, never applied** â€” the human edits `atlas.domains.json` globs to resolve it.

blocks.title: The pieces, in depth
blocks.intro: A full section per piece â€” what it does, how it works, its files and exports, and how it connects to the rest of the toolkit.

components.name: CLI
detail: The single entry point with three modes (paths may be relative â€” they resolve against the cwd). **Full scan** (`--repo <dir> --out <dir>`) loads or first-guesses `atlas.domains.json`, scans the repo, reconciles + reports drift, writes the config, drafts `atlas.json` and each `domain-<slug>/domain-<slug>.json` **only when absent** (or always with `--force`), then renders every doc. **Single-domain** (`--domain <slug>`) rescans and regenerates just that page (and prints a tile-update note). **Render-only** replays committed JSON â€” `--all <dir>` re-renders the atlas plus every `domain-<slug>/` folder, `--blocks <file>` renders one page.

`writeDraftIfAbsent` is the no-clobber guarantee: it skips any existing draft unless `--force`. The layout puts `atlas.{html,json}` + `atlas.domains.json` at the top of the out dir, and each domain self-contained in its own `domain-<slug>/` folder (page + diagram sidecars). It also warns about orphaned `domain-*/` folders left after a regroup â€” but never deletes files. Every mode except `--blocks` also refreshes `atlas-check.mjs` in the out dir (`emitChecker` copies the tool-owned `assets/atlas-check.mjs`, always overwritten) â€” the self-contained drift checker target repos commit and run from pre-commit/CI with plain Node.

exports.name: --repo
exports.desc: Full scan: config + drift + draft-when-absent + render.

exports.name: --domain
exports.desc: With `--repo`: rescan + regenerate one domain page.

exports.name: --all
exports.desc: Re-render the atlas + every `domain-<slug>/` from committed JSON.

exports.name: --blocks
exports.desc: Render one committed page JSON without rescanning.

exports.name: --force
exports.desc: Overwrite existing draft JSON (default: never clobber).

connections.body: `atlas.domains.json`, draft JSON (when absent), the rendered `.html`/`.json` per page, and `atlas-check.mjs` (drift checker, always overwritten)

components.name: config + drift
detail: `atlas.domains.json` is the human-owned source of truth: each `DomainConfig` carries a `slug`, `name`, and `globs` (the editable lever); the scanner fills the resolved `modules`. `firstGuessConfig` seeds a fresh config â€” one domain per immediate child directory of each srcRoot â€” so a new repo starts grouped, not blank. `reconcile` refills each domain's `modules` from its `globs` against the live inventory while **preserving every human-owned field**.

Crucially, `reconcile` reports **drift** but never applies it: `newModules` (in the repo, matched by no glob), `stalePaths` (config paths no longer present), and `emptyDomains` (globs resolving to nothing). The human resolves drift by editing globs. `matchGlob` is the tiny anchored matcher â€” `**` spans segments, `*` spans within one.

`AtlasConfig.exclude` is an optional list of globs dropped from the scan entirely â€” the case that needs it is an atlas whose out-dir lives inside the repo it documents (`docs/atlas/**`), whose own generated `atlas-check.mjs` would otherwise be reported as an unassigned module. The standalone checker honours the same field, since the two must agree or an excluded file reads as unassigned in one and absent in the other.

exports.name: firstGuessConfig()
exports.desc: Seeds a config: one domain per srcRoot subdir.

exports.name: reconcile()
exports.desc: â†’ `{ config, drift }` â€” refills modules, reports drift, never auto-applies.

exports.name: matchGlob()
exports.desc: The anchored glob matcher â€” `**` spans segments, `*` within one.

exports.name: AtlasConfig
exports.desc: The config shape (`repo` / `srcRoots` / `domains`).

connections.body: `atlas.domains.json` â€” the grouping config the human edits

components.name: scan + draft
detail: The hub of the [full-scan flow](#arch). `scanInventory` walks the srcRoots, and per module records resolved in-repo import keys, exported names, and whether it is a tRPC router â€” skipping generated/test trees and co-located test files (`*.test`/`*.spec` for TS/JS, and Python's inverse `test_*.py` / `conftest.py` / `_test.py` convention). It also collects Prisma model names. `aggregateDomainEdges` rolls moduleâ†’module imports up to cross-domain slugâ†’slug edges (intra-domain edges dropped).

Language routing happens here: a `.py`/`.pyi` module goes through [python-imports](../domain-code-intel/domain-code-intel.html#c-python-imports), everything else through the TS-compiler parsers. Python forces the scan into **two passes** â€” a Python absolute import can only be classified in-repo vs stdlib/third-party once every module key is known, so `.py` specifiers are stashed during the walk and resolved against the full known-module set afterwards. TS/JS edges still resolve inline in the first pass.

`buildAtlasDraft` emits the atlas-page doc: a tile per domain (with file counts and dep edges) plus the mechanical `domainMapDiagram`. `buildDomainDraft` groups a domain's modules by subdirectory into component cards + deep sections, listing real files and exports â€” but leaves every `purpose`/`detail` field **blank** for the human. The scanner never writes prose.

A `"."` srcRoot (a repo whose entry point sits at the top level) yields plain repo-relative paths rather than `./`-prefixed ones â€” the prefix would match neither the globs users write nor the paths `atlas-check.mjs` derives, splitting scanner and checker into silent disagreement.

exports.name: scanInventory()
exports.desc: â†’ `Inventory` â€” modules (imports/exports/router) + Prisma models.

exports.name: aggregateDomainEdges()
exports.desc: â†’ cross-domain slugâ†’slug import edges.

exports.name: buildAtlasDraft()
exports.desc: â†’ `AtlasDraft` skeleton (blank prose).

exports.name: buildDomainDraft()
exports.desc: â†’ `DomainDraft` skeleton (blank prose).

connections.body: [config](#c-config) â€” the grouped `AtlasConfig` to index modules to domains
the draft docs the [CLI](#c-cli) writes when absent

components.name: block model
detail: The type backbone of every atlas page â€” and the file this very section documents. The `AtlasBlock` union has nine members: atlas-page blocks (`atlas-tldr`, `domain-map`, `domain-index`), domain-page blocks (`domain-tldr`, `components`, `depth`, `owns`, `seams`), and the shared `diagram-section`. `ComponentDeep` is one expandable depth section; `AtlasDiagram` is a rendered d2/mermaid pair.

Beyond the types it carries page-option shapes (`AtlasOpts`/`DomainOpts`), `LAYER_DOTS` (the layer â†’ fill;stroke color table), and small helpers: `collectAtlasDiagrams` pulls every rendered diagram in document order, `assertUniqueAtlasIds` enforces unique block/component/diagram ids, and `isAtlasChapter`/`atlasChapterLabel` drive the numbered chapter nav.
components.codeHtml:
export type AtlasBlock =
  | AtlasTldrBlock | DomainMapBlock | DomainIndexBlock
  | DomainTldrBlock | ComponentsBlock | DepthBlock
  | OwnsBlock | SeamsBlock
  | DiagramSectionBlock;

files.name: atlas-blocks.ts
files.desc: The `AtlasBlock` union, `AtlasDiagram`/`ComponentDeep`, opts, `LAYER_DOTS`, and the id/diagram helpers.

exports.name: AtlasBlock
exports.desc: The nine-member block union every page is a list of.

exports.name: ComponentDeep
exports.desc: One expandable depth section.

exports.name: AtlasDiagram
exports.desc: One rendered d2/mermaid diagram.

exports.name: collectAtlasDiagrams()
exports.desc: Every rendered diagram, in document order.

exports.name: assertUniqueAtlasIds()
exports.desc: Id-uniqueness guard for block/component/diagram ids.

exports.name: LAYER_DOTS
exports.desc: The layer â†’ fill;stroke color table.

exports.name: AtlasOpts
exports.desc: Atlas-page option shape (title, chips, meta).

exports.name: DomainOpts
exports.desc: Domain-page option shape (layer, path, backHref).

connections.body: the atlas block model â€” every [domain](#c-gather) draft and [render](#c-assemble) is typed by it

components.name: render
detail: Turns a block list into a self-contained HTML page. `assembleAtlas` renders the map page (lead, domain-map, tile index); `assembleDomain` renders a domain page (lead, components, internal-arch, depth, owns, seams). `renderAtlasBlock` dispatches per block type, `renderAtlasDiagram` emits each diagram's d2/mermaid render with its authored legend, and `atlasLegend` builds the legend rows.

Every diagram is rendered through the diagrams domain up front (collected via `collectAtlasDiagrams`), then looked up by id while rendering. The output reuses the review app-shell/CSS from the recap domain â€” including the same inlined dark-mode toggle (`theme-head.js` head-apply script, `theme-toggle.js` button, `theme.css` appended last in `<style>`) â€” so atlas pages share the toolkit's look and theme. The CLI calls these two functions and writes the HTML next to the page JSON.

exports.name: assembleAtlas()
exports.desc: â†’ HTML for the map page.

exports.name: assembleDomain()
exports.desc: â†’ HTML for a domain page.

exports.name: renderAtlasBlock()
exports.desc: Per-block dispatch, wrapped in its `<section>`.

exports.name: renderAtlasDiagram()
exports.desc: Per-diagram render: svg box + legend + caption.

connections.body: [recap](../domain-recap/domain-recap.html) â€” the review app-shell + `review.css` token styles

components.name: completeness lint
detail: The demo-standard floor, mirroring the spec/recap linters: heuristics surfaced via `onWarn`, never hard errors. `lintAtlas` checks the atlas page leads with a `atlas-tldr` 'Start here', shows a domain map, and has a `domain-index` whose tiles all have a real `purpose` â€” flagging any blank ones the scanner left.

`lintDomain` checks a domain page opens with a `domain-tldr`, lists its `components`, includes an internal-arch `diagram-section` when it has four-plus deep components (`LARGE_COMPONENTS`), and names its `seams`. The warnings tell the human exactly which scanner-drafted fields still need enriching â€” closing the hybrid loop.

exports.name: lintAtlas()
exports.desc: â†’ warnings: missing lead / map / index, tiles with no purpose.

exports.name: lintDomain()
exports.desc: â†’ warnings: missing lead / components / arch-when-large / seams.

connections.body: the [block model](#c-blocks) â€” inspects block types and tile purposes

blocks.title: Data it owns
blocks.intro: Atlas owns the page block model and the grouping config â€” the two contracts every atlas page is built from. The block types below are the shape of a page; `AtlasConfig`/`atlas.domains.json` is the human-owned grouping.

rows.name: AtlasConfig Â· atlas.domains.json
rows.desc: The human-owned grouping: domains as `slug`/`name`/`globs`, with scanner-resolved `modules`. The one lever for which files form which domain.

rows.name: atlas-tldr Â· domain-map Â· domain-index
rows.desc: The atlas-page blocks: the 'Start here' lead, the all-domains map, and the grid of domain tiles.

rows.name: domain-tldr Â· components Â· depth Â· owns Â· seams
rows.desc: The domain-page blocks: the lead, the component cards, the in-depth sections, the data-owned table (this block), and the neighbor seams.

rows.name: diagram-section
rows.desc: The shared rendered-diagram block â€” the atlas spine/map and a domain page's internal-arch.

rows.name: Inventory Â· ModuleInfo Â· Drift
rows.desc: The mechanical scan products: per-module imports/exports/router flags, Prisma models, and the reported config drift.

blocks.note: The block model lives in `atlas-blocks.ts`; the config types in `atlas-config.ts`. The scanner fills `modules` and the draft skeletons; a human owns `globs`, `purpose`, and `detail`.

blocks.title: Seams
blocks.intro: Exposes the atlas CLI and its block model; depends on the rest of the toolkit for parsing, rendering, diagrams, and the shared app-shell.

exposes.note: full scan: config + drift + draft + render
the page contract authored pages target

blocks.note: `gather-atlas` imports **code-intel** (`imports`/`dep-graph`/`trpc-parse`/`prisma-schema`) for the scan; `atlas-blocks` builds on **core**'s `DiagramBlock`/`DiagramKind`; `assemble-atlas` renders via **diagrams** and reuses the **recap** review app-shell + `review.css`. The dependency on the rest of the toolkit is by reuse, not control â€” atlas stays the driver.
